did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Multivariable Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) with Hybrid Gradient Boosting (incorporating XGBoost, LGBM, CatBoost) and Hybrid Attention Module for the detection and classification of multiclass non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC) using histopathological images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is labeled as a 'Full Length Article' and presents original experimental work. It details the design of a 'Multivariable Deep Convolutional Neural Network model' with 'Hybrid gradient boosting' and 'Hybrid attention module'. The study includes an 'Experimental setup', 'Dataset description' (Histopathology NMSC segmentation dataset), 'Results and discussion' with specific performance metrics (accuracy 96.8%, sensitivity 97.56%, etc.), and a 'Comparative analysis' against existing models, indicating it produces new results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Detection of multiclass non-melanoma skin cancer with multi-variable DCNN with hybrid gradient boosting optimizer
brief_summary: This study addresses the detection and classification of multiclass non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC) using histopathological images. The authors propose a Multivariable Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) model integrated with a Hybrid Gradient Boosting optimizer and a Hybrid Attention Module to enhance feature extraction and classification accuracy. The model was evaluated using the Histopathology NMSC Segmentation Dataset and the Skin Cancer (PAD-UFES-20) Dataset. Performance was assessed through comparative analysis against existing models such as EfficientNet, CNN, ANN, DKCNN, HGB-DCNN, and HAM-DCNN using metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-score. The proposed model achieved an accuracy of 96.8%, sensitivity of 97.56%, and F1-score of 97.13% on the Histopathology NMSC Segmentation Dataset. The results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms comparative models, offering improved diagnostic accuracy for NMSC detection.

all_task_types.target: Multiclass non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC) diagnostic categories (Basal cell carcinoma, Squamous cell carcinoma, Intraepidermal carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'multiclass non-melanoma skin cancer' detection and classification. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (BCC, SCC, IEC) using a Multivariable Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) with Hybrid Gradient Boosting. Performance is evaluated using classification metrics: accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: Histopathology NMSC Segmentation Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing histopathological images of non-melanoma skin cancer with hand-annotated segmentation masks. The images are stored in TIF format, and cancer margin data is available in CSV format. The segmentation masks classify color pixels into 12 tissue classes: Glands (GLD), Inflammation (INF), Hair Follicles (FOL), Hypodermis (HYP), Reticular Dermis (RET), Papillary Dermis (PAP), Epidermis (EPI), Keratin (KER), Background (BKG), Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), and Intra-Epidermal Carcinoma (IEC).

use.training: Used for training the Multivariable DCNN model (80% of the dataset).
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate split; the paper mentions an 80:20 split for training and testing.
use.testing: Used for testing the Multivariable DCNN model (20% of the dataset).
use.other: Used for comparative analysis against existing models (EfficientNet, CNN, ANN, DKCNN, HGB-DCNN, HAM-DCNN).

datasets_used.name: Skin Cancer (PAD-UFES-20) Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public skin cancer dataset containing images received from 1373 patients with skin lesions. It includes 2298 images (1641 of which are specified as part of the total count mentioned, with 'others' implied) classified into various skin lesion types. The dataset includes 26 features per file (e.g., patient_id, lesion_id, smoke, drink, age) stored as CSV documents. It contains training, validation, and testing sets.

use.training: Used for training the Multivariable DCNN model via K-fold cross-validation (K=10).
use.validation: Used for validation via K-fold cross-validation (K=10).
use.testing: Used for testing via K-fold cross-validation (K=10).
use.other: Used for comparative analysis against existing models (EfficientNet, CNN, ANN, DKCNN, HGB-DCNN, HAM-DCNN).

To compare the proposed Multivariable DCNN model against existing models (EfficientNet, CNN, ANN, DKCNN, HGB-DCNN, HAM-DCNN) using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics.

architecture_used.main_model: Multivariable Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) with Hybrid Gradient Boosting (HGB) and Hybrid Attention Module
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet101 and VGG16 (pre-trained models fused for feature extraction)

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Fusion of two pre-trained models (ResNet101, VGG16) with shape and texture features (GDP, LDP, WLD)
architecture_details.optimizer_integration: Hybrid Gradient Boosting (HGB) incorporating XGBoost, LGBM, and CatBoost

classification_or_output_head.type: Multiclass classification for Non-Melanoma Skin Cancer (NMSC)

preprocessing_techniques: FNLM (Fast Non-Local Means) Denoising algorithm to remove noise
Region of Interest (ROI) extraction to segment the specific area of the skin cancer image from the original image
conclusions: The proposed Multivariable Deep Convolutional Neural Network (DCNN) model with Hybrid Gradient Boosting and Hybrid Attention Module effectively detects and classifies multiclass non-melanoma skin cancer (NMSC).
The model achieved an accuracy of 96.8%, sensitivity of 97.56%, specificity of 96.03%, precision of 96.71%, and F1-score of 97.13% on the Histopathology NMSC Segmentation Dataset.
The proposed method outperforms comparative models including EfficientNet, CNN, ANN, DKCNN, HGB-DCNN, and HAM-DCNN in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F1-score.

The integration of Hybrid Gradient Boosting (incorporating XGBoost, LGBM, and CatBoost) and a Hybrid Attention Module reduces model complexity while enhancing performance and feature extraction capabilities.
The model demonstrates robustness through K-fold cross-validation (K=10) and varying training percentages, with optimal efficacy observed at 80% training data and 100 epochs.
The study addresses limitations of existing methods regarding positional detection, tissue potential, transparency, and handling of large or poor-quality image datasets.
important_remarks: The study lacks external validation; it relies exclusively on two public datasets (Histopathology NMSC Segmentation and PAD-UFES-20) without testing on independent, multi-center clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability.
The Histopathology NMSC Segmentation Dataset is very small (n=290 total images), which raises concerns about overfitting and the robustness of the reported high accuracy (96.8%).
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed model over comparative baselines.

The PAD-UFES-20 dataset includes melanoma and other benign lesions (e.g., Nevus, Seborrheic Keratosis) in its full collection, but the specific class distribution and handling of class imbalance within the subsets used for NMSC classification are not detailed.

The paper does not report computational complexity metrics (e.g., FLOPs, inference time, parameter count), making it difficult to assess the feasibility of real-time clinical deployment despite claims of reduced complexity.

The model's performance is evaluated only on histopathological images; there is no assessment of its applicability to dermoscopic or clinical photographs, which limits its immediate clinical relevance for non-invasive screening.
The preprocessing pipeline includes ROI extraction and denoising, but the specific algorithms or thresholds used for these steps are not fully detailed, posing a risk for reproducibility.
The comparative analysis uses percentage improvements over baselines rather than absolute metrics for all models in some sections, which may obscure the absolute performance gap.
